g.data-narration: Welcome to Performance Measurement in Computer Architecture. Let's explore how we quantify computer speed.
Not all computers are equal. A supercomputer prioritizes speed while embedded processors need low power. We need objective metrics.
g.text: Different computers serve different purposes - speed vs power vs cost.
We need quantifiable metrics to compare processors objectively.

g.data-narration: Performance is fundamentally about reducing execution time. A faster computer completes the same task in less time.
g.text: Performance = Reduction in time to execute a task
Faster Computer = Less Time for Same Task

g.data-narration: We measure performance in two ways: execution time (latency) for single tasks, and throughput for continuous work.
g.text: Total time to complete ONE task
Total work done PER UNIT TIME

g.data-narration: The fundamental CPU performance equation relates execution time to clock cycles and cycle time.
g.text: CPU Execution Time = CPU Clock Cycles × Clock Cycle Time

g.data-narration: Let's see this in action. A 2 GHz CPU has a 0.5 nanosecond cycle time. 4 billion cycles takes 2 seconds total.
CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) measures how many clock cycles each instruction needs on average. It's a key microarchitecture metric.
g.text: Average number of clock cycles each instruction takes to execute
Total CPU Clock Cycles = Instruction Count × CPI

g.data-narration: The complete CPU time formula combines instruction count, CPI, and clock rate. These are the three factors affecting performance.
g.text: CPU Time = (Instruction Count × CPI) / Clock Rate

g.data-narration: Comparing CPUs shows why GHz alone is misleading. CPU A has higher clock rate but worse CPI than CPU B - they end up equal!
g.text: Example: CPU A vs CPU B Comparison

g.data-narration: Amdahl's Law shows the maximum speedup from improving part of a system. The unimproved portion always limits overall gains.
g.text: Amdahl's Law: The Limits of Optimization
Speedup limited by the fraction that CANNOT be improved

g.data-narration: Even a 3x improvement on 40% of code yields only 1.36x overall speedup. This highlights why optimizing the common case matters!
Benchmarks are standardized programs for measuring performance. SPEC is the industry standard benchmark suite with real-world applications.
g.text: Standardized programs designed to test computer performance
Real applications: video encoders, database servers, compilers

g.data-narration: Let's summarize the key metrics: Execution Time (lower is better), Throughput (higher is better), CPI, and the CPU time formula.
Always look beyond GHz - consider CPI and workload. Amdahl's Law teaches sustainable gains come from improving common operations.
g.text: Conclusion: Look Beyond Just Clock Speed!
Consider CPI, instruction count, and optimize the common case for sustainable performance gains.

g.data-narration: Thanks for learning about Performance Measurement. These concepts form the foundation for analyzing and optimizing computer systems.
